_scenario: Toddler 18 months — Motor Development (60.0) AND Communication (58.5) both priority. Actual LLM content will vary; structure must match exactly.
child_summary: This 7-day plan addresses two priority areas for your 18-month-old in CONCERN mode: Motor Development (Z-score 60.0) and Communication (Z-score 58.5). Social Engagement is on-track. Each day dedicates 2 of 3 activities to the priority domains with structured, targeted skill-building that increases in difficulty across the week.

activities.title: Ball Roll & Chase
activities.description: Roll a soft ball and encourage toddler to chase and bring it back.
steps: Roll a soft ball and encourage toddler to chase and bring it back.
activities.tip: Observe how your child responds and note any new reactions today.

activities.title: Point & Name
activities.description: Point to common objects around the house, say the name, wait for imitation.
steps: Point to common objects around the house, say the name, wait for imitation.
activities.tip: Observe how your child responds and note any new reactions today.

activities.title: Peek-a-Boo Variations
activities.description: Use a cloth to hide your face and different objects. Vary pace and surprise.
steps: Use a cloth to hide your face and different objects. Vary pace and surprise.
activities.tip: Observe how your child responds and note any new reactions today.

activities.title: Obstacle Walk
activities.description: Place soft cushions on the floor and encourage toddler to step over or around them.
steps: Lay 3 soft cushions flat on the floor about 30 cm apart.
Hold toddler's hand and slowly walk toward the first cushion.
Say 'step over!' and lift your own leg to demonstrate.
Let toddler attempt each cushion — offer a hand if they wobble.
Repeat the course twice.
activities.tip: Stepping over objects builds leg coordination and the balance needed for running and stair climbing.

activities.title: Two-Word Request
activities.description: Ask toddler to make a two-word request ('more milk', 'big ball') before giving them what they want.
steps: Hold up two options — e.g. a cup of milk and a toy.
Ask 'what do you want?' and wait 5 seconds.
If they point or make a sound, say the two-word phrase clearly: 'more milk'.
Pause and wait for toddler to attempt the phrase before handing it over.
Praise any attempt — even one correct word counts.
activities.tip: Pausing before giving the object teaches toddlers that words get results — the most powerful communication lesson at this age.

activities.title: Follow the Leader
activities.description: Take turns being the leader — do a simple action and ask toddler to copy, then let them lead.
steps: Stand facing toddler and clap your hands twice.
Say 'your turn' and wait for them to clap.
Stamp your feet twice — wait for them to copy.
Then crouch down and say 'you be the leader now' — copy whatever they do.
activities.tip: Turn-taking games at 18 months build the social awareness that supports both play and conversation.

activities.title: Step Up & Down
activities.description: Practice stepping up onto a single low step and back down with support.
steps: Stand beside a single low step (e.g. a doorstep or stair) and hold toddler's hand.
Say 'up!' and let them lead the stepping-up motion.
Once up, say 'down!' and hold their hand as they step back.
Repeat 6 times, reducing how much you hold as they gain confidence.
activities.tip: Stair navigation at 18 months is a major motor milestone — each repetition builds leg strength and balance control.

activities.title: Picture Book Talk
activities.description: Open a picture book, point to one image at a time, and wait for toddler to name or make a sound for it.
steps: Sit beside toddler with a simple picture book.
Point to one clear image — e.g. a dog — and say its name once.
Turn the page and point to the next image. Wait 5 seconds for toddler to name it.
If they are silent, say the word once and move on — no pressure.
Do 5 pages.
activities.tip: Labelling pictures builds vocabulary faster than any other home activity — 5 minutes a day makes a measurable difference.

activities.title: Sharing Circle
activities.description: Sit in a circle with one other adult and pass a toy around, modelling sharing and waiting.
steps: Sit on the floor in a triangle with toddler and one other adult.
Hold a toy and say 'my turn' then play with it for 5 seconds.
Pass it to the other adult who says 'my turn' and plays 5 seconds.
Pass it to toddler. If they don't hand it back, gently say 'pass it' and hold your hand out.
Do 3 full rounds.
activities.tip: Waiting for a turn is a key social-emotional skill — toddlers need to see adults model it before they can do it.

activities.title: One-Foot Stand
activities.description: Hold toddler's hand and encourage them to lift one foot off the ground and hold for 2 seconds.
steps: Stand facing toddler and hold both their hands.
Say 'lift one foot!' and demonstrate with your own foot.
Support their weight as they attempt to lift one foot.
Count out loud: '1, 2' then lower.
Try both feet. Aim for 3 attempts each side.
activities.tip: Single-leg balance at this age activates the deep core and hip stabilisers that underpin all future movement skills.

activities.title: Action Word Play
activities.description: Do a simple action — jump, clap, run — and say the action word as you both do it together.
steps: Stand facing toddler.
Jump twice and say 'jump!' clearly as you do it.
Hold toddler's hands and help them jump while saying 'jump!'.
Clap hands and say 'clap!', then help them clap.
Repeat with 3 different action words.
activities.tip: Pairing movement with words creates a strong memory link — the body and brain learn the word together.

activities.title: Role Play Tea Party
activities.description: Pretend to pour tea and eat biscuits — narrate the whole scene and invite toddler to join in.
steps: Sit with toddler and a cup or small bowl.
Pretend to pour tea and say 'here's your tea!'.
Wait for toddler to take the cup.
Pretend to sip and say 'mmm, yummy!' — encourage them to copy.
Extend the scene: 'more tea?', 'too hot?', 'share some?'
activities.tip: Pretend play at 18 months is a window into imagination and language — narrating the scene teaches sentence structure naturally.

activities.title: Underarm Throw Practice
activities.description: Teach an underarm throw by rolling a ball, then progressing to a low toss into a basket.
steps: Place a washing basket or large bowl 1 meter away from toddler.
Hand toddler a soft ball and say 'throw it in!'.
Demonstrate a gentle underarm toss into the basket.
Help toddler's arm through the throwing motion if needed.
Move basket closer or further to match their ability. Do 6 attempts.
activities.tip: Throwing involves coordinating shoulder, elbow, and wrist in sequence — this is complex motor planning for an 18-month-old.

activities.title: What's in the Bag?
activities.description: Put 3 familiar objects in a bag and ask toddler to name each one as they pull it out.
steps: Place 3 common objects (spoon, toy car, sock) in a cloth bag.
Hold the bag toward toddler and say 'what's in the bag?'.
Let them reach in and pull out one object.
Wait 5 seconds for them to name it. If silent, say the word once.
Repeat for all 3 objects. Try a second round with new objects.
activities.tip: Surprise and curiosity make words stick — toddlers remember words better when they discover the object themselves.

activities.title: Dance Together
activities.description: Put on a short song and dance together — copy each other's moves and laugh together.
steps: Play a short, upbeat children's song.
Hold toddler's hands and sway side to side.
Let go and let toddler move freely — copy whatever they do.
Clap together at the end and say 'yay!'.
activities.tip: Shared joy through movement builds emotional connection and body rhythm — both support future communication.

activities.title: Kicking a Stationary Ball
activities.description: Place a soft ball still on the floor and encourage toddler to kick it toward a target.
steps: Place a soft ball 30 cm in front of toddler.
Point to a cushion 2 meters away and say 'kick it to the pillow!'.
Demonstrate by kicking the ball gently toward the target.
Let toddler approach and kick — hold their hand for balance if needed.
Fetch and repeat 6 times.
activities.tip: Kicking requires transferring weight onto one foot while the other swings — a major balance and coordination challenge.

activities.title: Describe the Feeling
activities.description: Touch different textures together — rough, soft, cold, warm — and say the word each time.
steps: Gather 3 items of different textures: a rough towel, a soft cloth, a cold spoon.
Touch the rough towel and say 'rough!'.
Take toddler's hand and touch the same item. Say 'rough!' again.
Repeat with each item.
On the second round, touch the item and wait — see if toddler says the word.
activities.tip: Describing sensory experiences gives toddlers the vocabulary to express how they feel — a key step in emotional communication.

activities.title: Parallel Play Alongside
activities.description: Sit beside toddler doing the same activity — building blocks — without directing or correcting them.
steps: Sit beside toddler with identical sets of blocks for each of you.
Start building your own tower silently.
If toddler looks at you, smile and keep building — no instructions.
Comment on what you see: 'I'm making a tall one!'.
When they knock theirs down, react warmly: 'oops! build it again?'
activities.tip: Parallel play teaches toddlers to share space and enjoy others nearby — the step before cooperative play.

activities.title: Unassisted Stair Climb
activities.description: Let toddler attempt 3 stairs alone while you spot — reduce hand-holding from earlier this week.
steps: Stand directly behind toddler at the base of 3 stairs.
Say 'you can do it — up you go!'.
Keep both hands out to spot but do not hold — let toddler self-initiate each step.
Only catch if they lose balance.
After 3 stairs, bring them down holding hands and repeat twice.
activities.tip: Removing your hand on Day 7 is the test of the week's motor work — celebrating their independence builds confidence for the next challenge.

activities.title: Tell Me the Story
activities.description: Hold up a familiar book and ask toddler to tell you what they see — you listen and expand their words.
steps: Open a familiar picture book to a page toddler knows well.
Point to the main image and say 'what do you see?'.
Wait 5 seconds — if they say one word (e.g. 'dog'), expand it: 'yes, a big dog!'.
Turn the page and ask again: 'what happened next?'.
Do 4 pages. Let toddler lead.
activities.tip: Expanding single words into short sentences — without correcting — is the single most effective language strategy for this age.

activities.title: Helper Role
activities.description: Give toddler a simple task to help you — carry a light item, put something away — and thank them.
steps: Hold a soft item (like a folded cloth) and say 'can you help me put this away?'.
Walk with toddler to where it belongs.
Let them place it themselves.
Say 'thank you so much! you are such a good helper!'.
Give them one more simple task.
activities.tip: Being a 'helper' builds toddlers' sense of identity and belonging — the foundation of social confidence.

general_tips: At 18 months with two priority domains, keep each session short (1-2 minutes max) and fun — frustration is the enemy of learning.
Expand every word your toddler says into a short sentence immediately — 'ball' becomes 'yes, red ball!' — without correcting them.
Build activities into existing routines (meal prep, bath time, getting dressed) so they happen daily without extra scheduling effort.
Celebrate effort, not outcome — a failed throw or a mispronounced word still earns a warm 'great try!'.
Both priority domains improve faster when addressed together — motor play that requires instruction combines both targets in one activity.
